decision_simulation: The real historical moment: 1850, newly unified Switzerland. Federal authorities must decide whether to force all 40+ cantons to abandon their own historic currencies (some centuries old, deeply tied to local identity) in favor of a single national franc, right after a bitter civil war had already tested the fragile new federal union. The outcome was genuinely uncertain: cantons could resist currency unification the same way some had resisted political unification, potentially reigniting the tensions that caused the Sonderbund War.Player choice: Mandate full currency unification immediately, or allow a longer transition period with cantonal currencies still valid.Historical resolution: Switzerland unified quickly — the last cantonal coin was a 1-rappen piece from Zurich dated 1848, with full cantonal-to-franc conversion completed by 1851–1852.

understand.economic_impact: Unifying 40+ competing cantonal currencies into a single franc dramatically reduced the transaction costs and confusion of doing business across Swiss cantons, laying groundwork for the country's later emergence as a global financial safe haven UBS history archive. The 1865 Latin Monetary Union further integrated the franc with major European economies under one silver standard Wikipedia: Latin Monetary Union.
understand.political_impact: Currency unification was only possible because of the political unification forced by the 1847 Sonderbund War and the 1848 federal constitution — money and political stability were directly linked in Switzerland's founding Wikipedia: Sonderbund War. Switzerland's later reputation for neutrality and financial stability, cemented by institutions like the Bank for International Settlements (Basel, 1930), made it a preferred refuge for capital during periods of global instability BIS official history.
understand.why_it_still_matters: Imagine your town had its own money, and the next town over had different money, and you had to do math in your head every single time you bought something from them. That was Switzerland before 1850 — more than 40 different local currencies, all at once. After a short, sharp war forced the country to finally unite politically, they did the same with money: one franc, for everyone. That decision to trade local pride for shared simplicity is exactly why, nearly 175 years later, people from all over the world still send their savings to Switzerland when they're scared about the future. Being trusted with other people's money for that long isn't an accident — it's the result of one small, once-divided country deciding, once, to actually agree on something.
